content.text: Milvus is an open-source, distributed vector database built specifically for billion-scale similarity search.  Originally developed at Zilliz and donated to the Linux Foundation AI in 2019, it is today one of the most widely deployed vector databases in production.  Its architecture separates storage from compute, making it uniquely suited to large-scale workloads where self-hosting is preferred over a managed service.  The Python client
, an in-process build that works without Docker -- making the same API usable from laptop to billion-scale cluster.
-- including one mandatory primary key field and one or more vector fields.  It is distinct from Weaviate (schema-driven with auto-embedding) and Pinecone (flat id/vector/metadata) in several ways:
HNSW, IVF_FLAT, IVF_PQ, IVF_SQ8, DISKANN, and FLAT. The choice determines the memory/recall/latency trade-off per collection.
a collection must be loaded into memory before searching and can be released when idle, enabling fine-grained memory management.
filter conditions are string expressions (e.g. 'views > 1000 and source == "ml-blog"') evaluated alongside ANN search.
a collection can be subdivided into named partitions; searches can be scoped to specific partitions for performance.
clients always compute embeddings externally and pass float vectors to Milvus.  This decouples the embedding model from the database.
a fully managed Milvus service on AWS/GCP/Azure, with the same PyMilvus API.

attrs.alt: Milvus deployment modes (Lite / Standalone / Cluster) and core data model showing Collection fields (primary key, FLOAT_VECTOR, VARCHAR, INT64, partitions) and supported index types (HNSW, IVF_FLAT, IVF_PQ, DISKANN, FLAT)

content.text: and runs entirely in the calling Python process.  It persists data to a local

file (SQLite-backed) and supports the full PyMilvus API with the exception of distributed features.  It is ideal for development, unit tests, and small-scale prototyping without any infrastructure setup.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient

# Milvus Lite: uri=local file path
client = MilvusClient('./milvus_demo.db')
print('Milvus Lite ready, db saved to milvus_demo.db')

Standalone runs Milvus, etcd (metadata store), and MinIO (object storage) in a single Docker Compose deployment.  It supports up to tens of millions of vectors on a single machine and is suitable for most production use cases that do not require horizontal scaling.

# Download the official compose file
curl -sfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/milvus-io/milvus/master/scripts/standalone_embed.sh -o standalone.sh
bash standalone.sh start
# Milvus listens on port 19530 (gRPC) and 9091 (HTTP)

The cluster mode separates query nodes, data nodes, index nodes, and a proxy layer.  It uses Apache Pulsar or Kafka as a write-ahead log, etcd for distributed metadata, and MinIO or S3 for segment storage.  This architecture allows independent scaling of each component and supports billions of vectors across multiple machines.

Every Milvus collection requires a schema.  The schema must include a primary key field (INT64 or VARCHAR) and at least one FLOAT_VECTOR or similar vector field.  Scalar fields (VARCHAR, INT64, FLOAT, BOOL, JSON) are stored alongside vectors and used for filtered search.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType

client = MilvusClient('./demo.db')  # Milvus Lite
# or: client = MilvusClient('http://localhost:19530')  # Standalone

# Define schema
schema = client.create_schema(
    auto_id=True,         # Milvus generates INT64 primary keys
    enable_dynamic_field=True,  # allow extra fields not in schema
)
schema.add_field('id',        DataType.INT64,        is_primary=True)
schema.add_field('embedding', DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR, dim=1536)
schema.add_field('title',     DataType.VARCHAR,      max_length=512)
schema.add_field('source',    DataType.VARCHAR,      max_length=256)
schema.add_field('views',     DataType.INT64)

# Define index on the vector field (required before loading)
index_params = client.prepare_index_params()
index_params.add_index(
    field_name='embedding',
    index_type='HNSW',
    metric_type='COSINE',
    params={'M': 64, 'efConstruction': 256},
)
# Scalar field index for fast filter (optional but recommended)
index_params.add_index(
    field_name='source',
    index_type='TRIE',  # efficient prefix/equality on VARCHAR
)

# Create collection
client.create_collection(
    collection_name='articles',
    schema=schema,
    index_params=index_params,
)
print(client.list_collections())  # ['articles']

With auto_id=True, Milvus assigns monotonically increasing INT64 primary keys.  To control IDs (e.g. preserve document IDs from your system), set auto_id=False and include the 'id' field in every insert payload.  IDs must be unique -- Milvus does not upsert by default; use delete + insert to update a record.
Data is inserted as a list of dictionaries (or columnar format) into a named collection.  Embeddings must be pre-computed externally.

import openai
from pymilvus import MilvusClient

client = MilvusClient('./demo.db')
oai = openai.OpenAI()

documents = [
    {'title': 'How HNSW works',   'source': 'ml-blog',  'views': 4200,
     'body': 'HNSW is a graph-based ANN algorithm.'},
    {'title': 'IVF Index Guide',  'source': 'ml-blog',  'views': 3100,
     'body': 'IVF clusters vectors into Voronoi cells.'},
    {'title': 'Milvus at Scale',  'source': 'zilliz',   'views': 5800,
     'body': 'Milvus supports billion-scale vector search.'},
]

# Embed all document bodies in one API call
resp = oai.embeddings.create(
    model='text-embedding-3-small',
    input=[d['body'] for d in documents],
)
embeddings = [item.embedding for item in resp.data]

# Build row-oriented insert payload
rows = [
    {
        'embedding': emb,
        'title': doc['title'],
        'source': doc['source'],
        'views': doc['views'],
        # 'body' is stored as a dynamic field (enable_dynamic_field=True)
        'body': doc['body'],
    }
    for doc, emb in zip(documents, embeddings)
]

result = client.insert(collection_name='articles', data=rows)
print(result)  # {'insert_count': 3, 'ids': [450892..., ...]}
For large imports, batch inserts in chunks of 1,000-10,000 rows.  After bulk import, call
to force segments to disk and ensure data is durable.  Milvus automatically flushes periodically, but an explicit flush before a search after a large import guarantees visibility.
Before searching, the collection must be
into memory.  This loads the HNSW graph (or other index) and the vector data into RAM on the query nodes.  The load operation is idempotent -- calling it on an already-loaded collection is a no-op.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient
import openai

client = MilvusClient('./demo.db')
oai = openai.OpenAI()

# Load the collection into memory
client.load_collection('articles')

# Embed the query
query_text = 'approximate nearest neighbour graph traversal'
q_resp = oai.embeddings.create(model='text-embedding-3-small', input=[query_text])
query_vec = q_resp.data[0].embedding

# ANN search
results = client.search(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=[query_vec],              # list of query vectors
    anns_field='embedding',        # which vector field to search
    limit=5,                       # top-K results
    search_params={'ef': 128},     # HNSW ef at query time
    output_fields=['title', 'source', 'views'],  # scalar fields to return
)

for hit in results[0]:  # results[0] = hits for query 0
    print(f"dist={hit['distance']:.4f}  {hit['entity']['title']}")
# dist=0.1524  How HNSW works
# dist=0.2981  IVF Index Guide

# Release when done (frees memory; data stays on disk)
client.release_collection('articles')

Milvus supports pre-filtering (applied before ANN) and post-filtering via a string expression language.  Expressions use Python-like syntax with field names, comparison operators, logical operators, and membership tests.

# Filter by source and minimum views
results = client.search(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=[query_vec],
    anns_field='embedding',
    limit=10,
    filter='source == "ml-blog" and views > 3000',
    output_fields=['title', 'views'],
)

# IN / NOT IN
results = client.search(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=[query_vec],
    anns_field='embedding',
    limit=5,
    filter='source in ["ml-blog", "zilliz"]',
    output_fields=['title', 'source'],
)

# Scalar-only query (no vector, like a SQL SELECT + WHERE)
rows = client.query(
    collection_name='articles',
    filter='views > 4000',
    output_fields=['id', 'title', 'views'],
    limit=100,
)
for row in rows:
    print(row['title'], row['views'])

Expression filtering is applied before ANN on the HNSW index when a scalar index exists on the filtered field.  Without a scalar index, the filter falls back to a brute-force scan of segment metadata.  Always build scalar indexes on frequently filtered fields using
Index Types and When to Use Them

HNSW is the right default for most production workloads that fit in memory.  DISKANN is the choice when the collection is too large to keep in RAM but HNSW-like recall is still required.  IVF_PQ provides the lowest memory footprint but at the cost of recall and build complexity.

Partitions are named logical subdivisions of a collection.  A search scoped to one or more partitions only touches the data in those partitions, reducing the number of candidate vectors and improving performance for heavily filtered workloads.

# Create partitions
client.create_partition('articles', partition_name='ml-blog')
client.create_partition('articles', partition_name='research')

# Insert into a specific partition
client.insert(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=ml_blog_rows,
    partition_name='ml-blog',
)

# Search only in specific partitions
results = client.search(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=[query_vec],
    anns_field='embedding',
    limit=5,
    partition_names=['ml-blog'],  # restrict search scope
    output_fields=['title'],
)

# List partitions
print(client.list_partitions('articles'))
# ['_default', 'ml-blog', 'research']
Forgetting to load the collection before searching.
Milvus raises an error if the collection is not loaded.  Add client.load_collection() after create or at application startup.
Not building a scalar index on filtered fields.
Without a TRIE or BITMAP index on VARCHAR/INT64 fields, filter expressions trigger full segment scans and slow down search significantly.
Using HNSW for billion-scale without enough RAM.
An HNSW index on 1B 768-dim vectors requires ~6 TB of RAM.  Use DISKANN or IVF_PQ instead.
Inserting without flushing and searching immediately.
Freshly inserted data may not be visible until the segment is sealed and indexed.  Call client.flush() before a time-sensitive search after bulk inserts.
Setting ef too low in HNSW search_params.
The default ef for search is 16 in some versions, which gives poor recall.  Set ef=64 or higher explicitly in every search_params dict.
Use Milvus Lite (uri='./data.db') for all development and unit testing.  Switch to Standalone or Zilliz Cloud by changing only the uri.
Set M=64, efConstruction=256 for HNSW at collection creation; these cannot be changed later.
Create scalar indexes (TRIE for VARCHAR, BITMAP for low-cardinality INT64) on every field used in filter expressions before loading the collection.
Load collections at service startup and keep them loaded; loading takes time proportional to index size.
Batch inserts in 1,000-10,000 row chunks.  Finer batches cause excessive segment creation overhead.
For multi-tenant applications, prefer partitions over separate collections to share the HNSW index infrastructure.
Use enable_dynamic_field=True to store extra fields (like body text) without adding them to the fixed schema -- they are stored as JSON and accessible in output_fields.
Milvus is an open-source, distributed vector database with a typed schema model.  Milvus Lite (in-process) and Standalone (Docker) share the same PyMilvus API.
Collections require an explicit schema with a primary key and at least one vector field.  Embeddings are always provided by the client -- Milvus does not auto-embed.
The collection must be loaded into memory before searching and can be released when idle.  This explicit lifecycle enables fine-grained memory management.
Multiple index types (HNSW, IVF_FLAT, IVF_PQ, DISKANN) trade off memory, recall, and latency.  HNSW is the best default for in-memory production use.
Scalar filtering uses a Python-like expression language applied alongside ANN search.  Scalar indexes (TRIE, BITMAP) are required for efficient filtering.
Partitions scope search to data subsets, improving performance for partitioned workloads such as per-source or per-date data.
